scope.summary: F-LOW DI4: the compatibility-grant store evicted by arbitrary HashMap order once at capacity, and did so silently. Eviction is now oldest-first and observable.
out_of_scope: The 128-grant capacity itself and single-use redemption, both unchanged.
The canonical oracle_execute grant path; this is the compatibility execute_approved token store only.
Persisting grants across restart: they remain process-local by design.

entry_points.name: prune_execute_approved_tokens / oldest_execute_approved_key
entry_points.trace: Eviction selects the earliest-expiring grant (uniform TTL, so earliest expiry is earliest issuance) instead of keys().next(). Expired grants are still pruned first. A live eviction now emits a warn carrying evicted count, capacity, retained count and the remediation (re-run preview_sql); counts only, because these are credential-adjacent deposits and the token text must never reach a log line. 346 oraclemcp lib tests pass. MUTATION CONTROL, which taught something: restoring keys().next() yields eviction order [k15,k05,k03,k06,k12,k02,...] — its FIRST pick happened to be the oldest, so the single-pick assertion PASSED BY LUCK; what kills the mutation is the drain-order assertion that repeated eviction proceeds strictly oldest-first. Test keys are assigned so age runs opposite to lexical order.

limitations: The selection is unit-tested through a pure seam (oldest_execute_approved_key) rather than by driving a live dispatcher: DispatcherState owns a boxed Oracle connection. The capacity loop and expiry pruning around it are covered by inspection, not by a test that fills the store to 128.
Ties are broken arbitrarily among grants sharing an expires_at instant; grants issued in the same instant are the same age, so no ordering guarantee is claimed there.
Implemented in a worktree at HEAD because another pane held ~1081 uncommitted lines in dispatch/ at the time; the commit therefore contains only these two files.
